sentences.text: I walked in and I said, I'm going to beg for bread.
I opened the door and there was like a hallway.
And I see there is a pair of rubber boots, high boots standing there.
And then is hanging a jacket with a P on it.
That means that a Polack is working there.
But it was quiet.
I hear that they went to work in the morning, how they took the horses, and they went out with the horse and buggy that I heard that it's going out.
So I'm thinking, shall I go for the bread, or shall I take the boots and the jacket and get out of here?
I took the boots and the jacket into my department, where the pigs were, and I tried them on because I had frozen feet, because we were marching those three weeks.
My feet were frozen and my fingernails came off.
And I had them wrapped around with part of the blanket that I tore up, that I couldn't work anymore.
I tried them on.
It was a little bit tight, but I put them on and I put on the jacket, and I walked out from this house.
And I walk on the street-- it was in a country-- and I seen a guy going by that he has also a P.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was riding on a horse and buggy-- a young fellow with a P.
I say, halt.
I ask him, could you help me, please?
I am a Polack.
I was afraid to say that I am a Jew.
He said, what are you doing?
I says, I was in concentration camp.
Why were you in concentration camp?
I says, my father and my mother did not want to sign that they are Volksdeutsche.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they killed them and they put me in concentration camp.
And then they bombed the camp, and I ran away.
Could you please hide me?
I'm a Polack.
Do something for your country.
Help me.
So he take me into the place where he was working.
He was a forced laborer also, that they took in from Poland, but he was free.
He was working for a farmer.
But he was free.
So he took me and he hide me in his barn on top, where the cows were.
And every day, he brought me in a sandwich.
But I was hungry.
A sandwich was not enough for me a whole day.
So I went-- when they went to work, I went down from the barn to the cows, and tried to get a little bit milk.
So I go under there.
I don't know to work milk into my mouth, so it ran into my eye, or into my ear.
I couldn't aim it.
It was such a hard time.
Finally, I found a bottle.
I try to milk into the bottle.
It was terrible.
It was no food enough for me.
I was so hungry that I start walking across the yard and steal boiled potatoes for the pigs.
Finally, the grandma saw me in that yard, that somebody is there.
So the Polack comes in one day.
He says, get out from here.
You must go out.
The old woman-- she thinks the partisans are here in the courtyard, here.
She's seen somebody walking across the courtyard.
The potatoes are missing, the cows don't have any milk.
You have to get out of here.
They're going to come and hide the whole-- clean up the whole place in here.
A gendarme is going to come here.
Whether I wanted to go or not, I was pushed out.
And I see that there's a group of Poles over there, and I say what the-- he says, go with that group that outside.
They're evacuating also, because the Western front was pushing to east.
Everybody was evacuating to east.
I stay, and I don't know whether I should go, or I don't go.
I am on the arms already.
He say go.
You have to go.
And I go.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] On the highway we walked maybe two, three hours, and then they come-- the English airplanes-- [GERMAN].
No, not [GERMAN].
Spitfires.
And they start shooting with machine guns.
So as soon as they start shooting with machine guns, everybody runs in the forest this way and this way, out.
I was running very far out in the forest, and I drop my pants, and I sit there maybe for about three hours until everybody went away, until it got quiet, and I start walking backwards.
As I walk backwards, I was afraid to walk with the street.
I was walk with the fields.
I finally come into a town from the back, and I was begging food.
They said, what are you doing here?
I says, I'm a Polack.
I was in the Polish camp, and they bombarded the camp.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm looking for work.
Please give me work.
I work for free, just for food.
Please, I was in the Polish camp.
They bombarded the camp.
So they give me a sandwich, and I seen that she sent out something-- a young boy-- something.
I don't know.
And I gobbled in that sandwich, and I said, do you have work for me?
Can I stay with you?
Yeah, well, maybe we'll have work.
You want another sandwich?
I said yes.
So I take the other sandwich, and then as soon as I-- I wanted to go out.
As soon as I come out, that little boy comes back with a policeman.
[GERMAN] I say, [GERMAN] a camp-- in a Polish camp, I says I was.
And they bombarded the camp.
And I want some work.
Well, did they evacuate it, the whole camp, he says.
I says, I have frozen feet.
I could not walk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN] He says there were horse and buggies to go on.
I said they didn't let me on.
That was only for older people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they asked, go to the Burgermeister.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] How do you say Burgermeister in English?
To the mayor of the town.
Maybe he has some work for you.
So they go to the mayor of the town.
He say, going to find this woman, and go over.
He sent over the little boy to this woman.
Nobody needed me.
The boy comes back.
He says, I go with him to this and this place.
Maybe they'll take him.
And then the father told him to go to another place, also.
And I come back, the police man goes back to his place from the mayor.
And he ask me with that little boy, so did you find already work for him?
He says, no, they don't need him.
[GERMAN].
He says, [GERMAN].
He has to ask for another place.
Maybe they'll have work.
I want to work for free.
And the police man says then, [GERMAN] I say, [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] he says.
He knew right away that I am a Jew, but he says, go already.
I went to the other place.
They still didn't have any job for me.
So he finally gave me a sandwich.
He says, go to the other town and tell them that I sent you.
I said, maybe you want to give you a little note, because I wouldn't know how to repeat.
He says, no, I'm not going to give you any note.
Just tell them that you were here and I send you.
As I walked out, the children were running after me like I would be a crazy man, something like this.
And I walked with the fields, through the fields, across the other town.
I come to the other town.
I seen a few Polacks sitting on the bench.
And I tell them the same story-- that I am a Polack, and my father did not want to sign Volksdeutsche.
They put me in the camp, and the camp was bombarded, and I'm looking for work.
He says, oh, you come with me.
I'm working for the mayor and he has to give you a job.
So I went with him to the mayor.
The mayor don't have any, but he called somebody else.
She didn't want to take me.
She don't have any room.
I say, I'm going to sleep with the cow, together.
Please give me a job.
OK, sleep with the cow.
Sleep with the cow.
I was hiding a whole month.
I was sleeping with the cow there and I got the job.
And every day, I worked out in the field-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until the Russians came to this town.
It was [GERMAN].
I have a picture of that woman where I ran away.
If you want to stop the camera, I'll show you the picture.
Yeah.
And then the Russians came in and I asked her to give me a couple shirts, and they gave me a few sandwiches.
I want to go home.
How do I go home when there was no transportation at that time?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russians took me to a barn there, and they gave me food, what they cooked, and they gave me a glass of vodka.
It tasted like rubbing alcohol, and I had to drink this.
I vomit out everything that I ate a month ago.
And then they gave me a bicycle and I went home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the way home, I was riding with the bicycle on the Autobahn, and that bicycle was exchanged with me 10 times, until I finally got a bike that it was egg-shaped.
That it didn't go anymore.
I had to throw it away.
And the exchange was with Russian soldiers.
He just comes over there.
He says, give me your bike.
I give-- give me yours, I give you mine.
And if you don't want to give it to them, he says, hey, [RUSSIAN].
I liberated you.
You have to give it to me, what I say.
Otherwise you're getting with the machine gun from me.
So, finally, I throw away the bike and we got on the boxcars, but they went a train that went to Moscow.
And we went on that train and were riding all night until it went through Poznan.
And I went off Poznan and I transferred to another train that goes to Katowice, and from Katowice I went into Bedzin.
I come into town-- to my hometown.
I remember, I walk on the street and I don't know where to go.
I don't see a single Jew.
I thought I am the only Jew that survived.
And I go the place where it was the Jewish Community Center, and I look up and, finally, one guy comes by on a bike and he says to me, [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He says, come with me.
And he took me to the place where-- there was another place-- a small community center where there were maybe 30 or 35 Jews in the whole town.
And they gave me 50 zlotys, and they give me some cards to buy food-- stamps to buy food-- because without stamps you couldn't get a ration card, like rations.
Without this you couldn't get-- and he says, you can sleep in this and this place, this [INAUDIBLE] where there were beds like for the army, you know?
Like a barracks.
Yeah, like a barracks.
But this was a high-rise building.
It used to be the City Hall before there.
So I go to sleep over there.
I found-- my friends came in next day, Abe Frosch and his brother and [INAUDIBLE] came in, and we went to school together.
So these were my closest that we found each other.
We were there maybe a week in Bedzin, and we had to stay to go to the orphanage house for soup.
And, from the ration cards, with 50 zlotys what could you buy?
Practically it was not enough.
So we went back to Germany, maybe to find somebody or to get something or-- so went back to-- we went to Breslau.
We go back to Katowice and we jump on the boxcars, and we did not know where the boxcar is going.
It's a freight train.
And we jumped on it and we go west.
We did not know where it's going even.
And we went to Opole.
In Opole, the train station doesn't go any further.
So we start walking.
There was no transportation.
We finally see a Russian tractor going with a trailer, so we all three sit down in the back of it.
There was a board in the back of the trailer-- between the trailer and the tractor.
We sit down on it, and we were riding.
And the Russians seen us, and they came out.
Hey, what are you doing here?
So we told them what it's all about.
We were riding with them a whole week, and they cook.
They grabbed the chicken from the Germans.
Most of the Germans ran away, and we kind of stayed with them together until we got into Breslau.
We got into Breslau, there were empty apartments.
And we walked from apartment to apartment, looking for food, the three of us.
So we were just like thieves.
We walk in only in the kitchen.
We see a little bit of oatmeal.
We put in this stocking.
And a little of the sugar, we put it in this stocking.
A little bit of flour, we put it in this stocking.
We go to the next apartment.
We do the same thing until we filled it up.
There was no market where to shop or to buy anything.
Everything was bombed.
Was shrapnel all over the ground.
Until we fill this up and we say, OK, we're going to stay this apartment.
We're going to stay.
We grabbed everything into this apartment-- some records and a record player-- because everything was abandoned.
Everything was empty.
So we said, we're going to stay there.
And we stayed there maybe about three months and my friend, Jack Frosch, found out that his cousin is alive and, finally, she came and she lived with us together another two months.
And then--
--her uncle--
Fela Gipsman, which is now my wife.
Her uncle came, which he lived on the west side.
He found out again that we are alive, and he took us to Munich.
We went through Prague through the-- we couldn't go through.
We had to smuggle ourselves into the west zone.
First we went to Sosnowiec.
I found out that my father isn't buried in Belsen.
That he lives.
And so we went all to the west side, and we went to Munich.
In Munich I kind of separated with my wife, and I went to look for my father.
I went to Bergen-Belsen, which he was in the English zone.
I wanted that my wife should go with me.
I weren't married.
Yes.
She should come with me, and the uncle said, oh, no.
You get married, you go.
You cannot go.
So she stayed with the uncles and with the Frosches there in Munich and I went to Bergen-Belsen.
And I found my father.
I was with my father there in the camp maybe two weeks, and he found a girlfriend and he remarried later on also.
And he had two children.
But as soon as I was there two weeks in Bergen-Belsen, I went back.
I wanted to go back to Munich to stay in Munich, you know, with my friends and with my girlfriend.
But on the way I found another friend on the train.
He said, we'll go by Fuerth.
And there is a German Community Center, which they give ration cards and they give some money, and they give food.
So let's go down in meantime here because we were always hungry.
So we go to the Center, and we tell them that we are survivors.
They gave us ration cards and they gave us some money, and they asked us if we want to stay overnight here.
We said, yes, we're going to have a place to sleep.
And then they asked us if we want to work, so yes.
So there is a Jewish major working on the American Air Force Base.
And she gave us address where to go, and he gave me a job there, me and my friend.
As an electrician I worked, and he went into the kitchen to work.
I worked there as an electrician.
He worked in the kitchen.
I was jealous.
He's going to have all the food, and I don't.
I go back, and to the major I say, I want to work in the kitchen.
He's laughing.
He says, you've got a better job.
You have a good job.
You're going to have all the food you want.
No, I want to work in the kitchen.
But he's peeling potatoes.
That's all he does.
You want to work on KP when you already have such an electrical job, and Germans are working for you?
I says, no, I want to work in the kitchen.
I don't want to be a foreman.
So I was better off.
He put me back in the kitchen peeling potatoes because I was-- so we peel one potato, one orange.
Then we were eating all day long until we got diarrhea.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
From the food over there, from the quick change of food I were there, I got sick.
And my skin started to dry up and got so stiff, and they cracked.
And it start itching terrible.
It itched so hard that I could not work anymore.
I was in the displaced persons camp in Fuerth.
They will open up later a camp and I was in the camp.
I could not work anymore.
They finally took me to the hospital there, and then they transferred me to Nuremberg Health Clinic, a special clinic for skin disease.
I was laying in the hospital there maybe six weeks.
Every week the skin got stiff, cracked, and it came off.
And you could sweep away a shovel of flakes every day from under my bed.
It was itching so terrible.
I was running out naked outside to freeze for the itch because it was itching so terrible.
I didn't know what to do with myself.

Finally, the American doctor and the German and the doctor from the camp, which was also Polish, came with the military Red Cross van and they took me-- this was ambulance-- they took me to Erlangen Universitat Health Clinic-- university skin school.
In that hospital they make all kinds of tests for me.
They put on different kind of ointments, and they mark down to see which one it heals better.
They made all kinds of-- finally, they decided to give me injections that I should get temperature.
And I got temperature a few times, and they give me baths.
It's got a little bit better.
Everything healed except where I was bending.
It didn't want to heal.
Then they sent me for x-rays, special x-rays.
It finally healed up.
And from that time I went to Stuttgart where my father was.
He was in the camp at Stuttgart.
I stayed in Stuttgart in a camp and I was already recuperating from the hospital.
I got a job in the camp as administrator in the Bialik School.
I worked there in the camp.
And in between we went back and forth to Munich.
I see my girlfriend and visit my friends.
Every month I went to Munich to visit her.
And finally after two years in camp in 1947 we decided to get married.
We married in Munich was the wedding.
And then I took her to Stuttgart and we lived in a camp where I had-- in that school I had a separate room upstairs.
And we lived there two more years.
Finally, they put a sign in the camp.
Anybody who wants to go to the United States should register in the office at this and this time because President Truman signed a law to let in 400 displaced persons to this country.
We thought it's a joke, but we registered.
And as soon as we registered then they called us to CIC for checking, and we had to bring two witnesses that we were in camp and I showed the number.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they registered everything was OK.
They call us three weeks later to come to the doctor to check us out, and the dentist.
And then they call us to come with a suitcase to another camp to go to Ludwigsburg.
We were in Ludwigsburg a whole week, sitting in that camp.
It was like to make ready to go to a ship, a camp for they came.
And from there they took us to Hamburg-- to Bremenhaven-- from Bremenhaven to Hamburg.
From Hamburg we went on the ship We were 10 days on the ship, and we came into New York.
We came into New York.
They didn't let in the ship because it was Saturday, something with the union or strike-- they don't want to pay double times.
So, finally, Monday morning, they unloaded us.
And two women came from the Jewish Displaced Person Committee or something--
The Jewish Relief Organization.
A Jewish Relief Organization.
Yes.
And they said-- we supposed to go to Reading, Pennsylvania, and they say, no, you're not going to Reading, Pennsylvania.
You're going to Lancaster, Pennsylvania.
I said, I'd like to stay in New York.
No, you cannot stay in New York, but if you are on your own, if you have money, you can stay.
I says, all I have is $5, what they gave me on the ship.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They gave us on the ship $5 each person to buy Coca-Cola or cigarettes.
That's all I have.
And so they took us for lunch in a drugstore I remember.
And they asked me, what do you want to eat?
I want an egg.
I said, OK, an egg.
What kind of egg?
I says, not-- egg-shaped egg.
[LAUGHS] They meant whether scrambled.
And I cook it, boil it, whatever.
It's OK.
With bread?
Yes, bread.
What kind of bread?
What kind of bread?
Bread is bread.
We didn't have any what kind of bread in Europe.
We had bread, but only one kind.
And we ate this food and then they put us on the train, and we couldn't speak English with a tag on it.
And, finally, we were on the train.
And the conductor comes over.
Get off.
It's Lancaster, Pennsylvania.
So everybody gets off from the train, and we stayed.
We don't know where to go.
Finally, everybody left and the two guys staying-- two people were staying, and they pointed to us, saying that must be them, probably.
So they took us and they came down and they gave us a furnished room.
And, after a week, my wife got a job to work sewing on a machine for little dresses to make to sew.
And for me, they couldn't find a job as an electrician, so they put me-- ask me if I want to work in a general cigar company to nail boxes together.
I said, anything.
I don't want any charity.
I want to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I worked there in the general cigar company.
I finally got in as a maintenance man helping the other electrical work and any other kind of work there.
And I worked there about three months, and I found out that I have a cousin working in Detroit.
And I was making $0.75 an hour, and in Detroit, he was working as a conveyor loader.
He made $1.35 an hour.
So I say, there's no place for me here.
I asked the guy, if I work in the cigar company let's say 10 years, and I am the best, how much will I make?
He says, you will make maybe $60 a week.
I says, I quit.
He says, why don't you look for the cake and don't throw away the bread first?
I says, if I don't throw away the bread, I have no time to look for the cake.
So I quit without anything, and I went home.
And we finally went to Detroit.
In Detroit, I've--
